answer:
- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007. Meta-analysis finds that intrinsically motivating job features—autonomy, task variety, feedback, task significance, and complexity—strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously, especially in knowledge work.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010. Pay is positively related to job and pay satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This suggests salary matters, but usually less than people expect once pay is already high.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis”** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014. Intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially for complex and creative work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, but they do not replace interest in the work itself.

- **“The ‘What’ and ‘Why’ of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior”** — Deci & Ryan, 2000. The paper argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. “Interesting work” matters most when it also lets you feel ownership, mastery, and connection.

- **“A Review of Self-Determination Theory’s Basic Psychological Needs at Work”** — Van den Broeck, Ferris, Chang & Rosen, 2016. Need satisfaction at work is strongly associated with engagement, job satisfaction, and lower burnout. The evidence favors optimizing for intrinsically motivating environments, but not interest alone.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005. Person–job fit and person–organization fit are strongly related to satisfaction, commitment, and performance. This implies that alignment with the actual work and culture may matter more than prestige or title.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis”** — Fried & Ferris, 1987. Core job characteristics such as skill variety, autonomy, task identity, task significance, and feedback predict satisfaction and internal motivation. The findings support choosing roles with richer, more meaningful work design.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion”** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006. People tend to overestimate how much higher income will improve day-to-day happiness. This is directly relevant when comparing a modestly higher salary against a potentially less engaging role.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being”** — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010. Higher income is associated with better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being shows diminishing returns after a certain point. For already well-paid professionals, the marginal happiness gain from more salary may be limited.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved”** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023. This paper revises earlier income findings and shows that well-being often continues rising with income, though effects differ across people. It weakens any claim that money stops mattering entirely, but still does not imply salary should dominate the decision.

- **“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want”** — Wilson & Gilbert, 2005. People systematically mispredict how future events will affect their happiness, often overestimating the lasting impact of salient changes. This suggests caution about over-weighting either the excitement of leading a team or the loss of interesting research.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work”** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997. People who see their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and meaning than those who see it mainly as a job or career. The paper supports the idea that deep interest and meaning in work can be unusually important.

- **“The Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Job Performance: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review”** — Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton, 2001. Job satisfaction is positively related to job performance across studies. If interesting work substantially increases satisfaction, it may also support long-term performance and career success.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle”** — Benson, Li & Shue, 2019. The paper finds that organizations often promote top individual contributors into management, even when individual contributor skill does not predict managerial effectiveness. This is relevant because leading a team can be rewarding, but it is a different job rather than simply a better version of the old one.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report greater meaning and satisfaction. However, people differ in whether they primarily seek calling, career advancement, or income from work.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson**  
  Deeply meaningful work can produce high commitment and fulfillment, but also self-sacrifice and tolerance of worse conditions. This is a caution against treating interesting or meaningful work as the only criterion.

answer:

  This classic paper finds that work is more motivating and satisfying when it has skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. It supports the idea that “interesting” work matters, but it frames interest as one part of a broader job-design bundle.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000)**  
  This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness strongly predict motivation and well-being. It supports prioritizing intrinsically motivating work, but not “almost exclusively,” because social context and autonomy are equally central.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al. (2010)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction. It suggests salary increases may be less important than people expect, especially once compensation is already high.

- **“Vocational Interests and Job Performance: A Meta-Analysis” — Nye et al. (2012)**  
  This paper finds that fit between a person’s interests and their work predicts job performance, training performance, and persistence. It supports taking “interesting work” seriously because interest is linked not only to happiness but also to effectiveness.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that person–job fit and person–organization fit are strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. It implies that interesting tasks matter, but so do manager fit, team fit, and company values.

- **“Job Demands–Resources Theory” — Bakker & Demerouti (2017)**  
  This paper argues that engagement comes from job resources such as autonomy, feedback, support, learning, and growth, while burnout comes from excessive demands. It suggests “interesting work” is important, but sustainable motivation depends on the whole work environment.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)**  
  This study shows that people who see their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. It supports the value of meaningful work, while also showing that meaning is partly constructed rather than purely determined by the role.

- **“The Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski (2010)**  
  This review finds that meaningful work arises from multiple sources: the self, other people, work context, and broader purpose. It argues against reducing career choice to task interestingness alone.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**  
  This paper finds that higher income improves life evaluation, while day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns beyond a certain income level in their data. It suggests extra salary may matter, but less than role quality for daily experience.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth (2021)**  
  This paper challenges the idea of a sharp income plateau, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income. It weakens any claim that salary should be ignored, even if intrinsic work remains important.

- **“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003)**  
  This paper distinguishes harmonious passion, which supports well-being, from obsessive passion, which can create conflict and rigidity. It suggests that caring deeply about interesting work is beneficial, but focusing on it “almost exclusively” can become harmful.

- **“Happiness and Productivity” — Oswald, Proto & Sgroi (2015)**  
  This experimental paper finds that happier people are more productive in a controlled work-like setting. It supports choosing work conditions that sustain well-being, including interest, autonomy, and positive affect.**

reasoning:

I need to focus on providing only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries for the user's question about whether doing interesting work is underrated. Their friends claim that focus should be almost exclusively on interesting things, but I need to point out that salary, autonomy, and job fit also matter. The user doesn't want advice, just paper titles and summaries, so I'll start with Judge et al. 2010, which discusses the relationship between pay and job satisfaction.
**Summarizing research insights**

The correlation between pay satisfaction and actual pay with job satisfaction is weak to moderate, indicating that pay alone isn't the strongest predictor. One significant paper, by Humphrey et al. (2007), emphasizes that factors like autonomy and task significance affect satisfaction and motivation, though these are not the only predictors. Fried & Ferris (1987) discuss how job characteristics relate to satisfaction, focusing on the importance of meaningful work. Lastly, Deci et al. (1999) found that extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for engaging tasks, but context plays a crucial role.
**Highlighting key research findings**

In the paper by Cerasoli, Nicklin, and Ford (2014), it’s noted that intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, particularly regarding quality, while extrinsic incentives are more influential for quantity. Both factors work together to influence outcomes. Additionally, the work by Lyubomirsky, King, and Diener (2005) explores the relationship between happiness and success, suggesting that positive affect might not necessarily lead to success, which is an interesting perspective to consider in this context.
answer:
- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.**  
  Autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. This supports valuing interesting, meaningful work, but also shows social context and work structure matter.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris, 1987.**  
  Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback are associated with higher satisfaction and internal motivation. The evidence supports “interesting work matters a lot,” not “it is the only thing that matters.”

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.**  
  Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality and persistence on complex tasks. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the literature does not support ignoring salary or advancement entirely.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Tangible, controlling rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This suggests compensation and promotion incentives should be considered carefully, but not that they are always harmful.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.**  
  People perform better when their work fits their interests. For research-heavy roles, genuine intellectual interest is likely not just pleasant but performance-relevant.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and manager predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. “Interesting work” is one form of fit, but team, culture, and manager fit also matter substantially.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.**  
  Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, while pay satisfaction matters somewhat more. This weakens the case for moving mainly for a small salary increase.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010.**  
  Higher income is associated with better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in their data. This implies salary matters, but its marginal value may be limited once needs and comfort are met.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021.**  
  This paper found that experienced well-being continues to rise with income beyond the earlier proposed plateau. It complicates the “salary barely matters” story, though the gains are still typically smaller than people expect.

- **“A Collaborative Reanalysis of the Income–Happiness Relationship” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.**  
  The paper reconciles earlier conflicting findings by showing that income keeps helping many people, while the least happy group shows more plateauing. The takeaway is nuanced: money matters, but it is unlikely to dominate meaningful work for everyone.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher life and job satisfaction. This supports the idea that deeply meaningful or interesting work can be unusually valuable.

- **“The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review” — Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton, 2001.**  
  Job satisfaction is positively related to job performance, though the relationship is moderate rather than overwhelming. Enjoying and valuing the work likely helps, but it is not a complete substitute for role quality, growth, leadership, or incentives.

answer:
- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).**  
  Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially quality and complex-task performance. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence supports weighting interesting work heavily but not almost exclusively.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).**  
  People perform better and persist more when their vocational interests fit their work environment. This supports the idea that genuinely interesting work is instrumentally valuable, not merely a perk.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).**  
  Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. Interesting research work likely scores high on several of these, but team leadership might too if it gives autonomy and significance.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris (1987).**  
  Enriched job characteristics reliably correlate with satisfaction, motivation, and some performance outcomes. The evidence favors caring a lot about day-to-day work design rather than optimizing only for pay or title.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).**  
  Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This supports caution about trading curiosity and autonomy for compensation or status alone.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile (1997).**  
  Intrinsic task motivation is especially important for creativity, alongside challenge, autonomy, resources, and supportive management. This is highly relevant for research engineering, where creative problem-solving is central.

- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999).**  
  People tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by money and underestimate intrinsic motives. This supports the weaker claim that interesting work may be underrated relative to salary and status.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006).**  
  People often overpredict the happiness benefits of income because money becomes especially salient during comparisons. This suggests a modestly higher salary can be overweighted relative to daily work experience.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).**  
  Emotional well-being generally rises with income for most people, though the relationship varies and shows diminishing practical importance for some groups. This argues against ignoring salary, while still leaving room for interesting work to dominate at high income levels.

- **“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages” — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger (2023).**  
  Workers place substantial monetary value on nonwage job attributes such as autonomy, schedule control, paid time off, and meaningful work. This supports treating job content and working conditions as real compensation, not soft factors.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).**  
  Person–job and organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, turnover intentions, and performance. Your fit with research work and your fit with management responsibilities are both important, so the evidence does not privilege “interestingness” universally.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018).**  
  People who see interests as fixed are less open to new domains and less persistent when interests become difficult. This cautions against assuming the less-interesting leadership role cannot become interesting through growth.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue (2019).**  
  Strong individual contributors are often promoted into management, but individual-contributor excellence does not reliably translate into managerial success. If the Google role’s main attraction is leading a team, the key question is whether management itself fits you.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997).**  
  People who experience work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those seeing it mainly as a job or career. This supports taking meaning and interest seriously, but also shows that one’s orientation toward work shapes the experience.

answer:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson — “Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features” (2007).**  
  Meta-analysis showing that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. This supports the idea that interesting work matters a lot, but also shows that social context and working conditions matter too.

- **Fried & Ferris — “The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model” (1987).**  
  Classic meta-analysis finding that enriched, challenging jobs are associated with higher satisfaction and internal motivation. The evidence favors intrinsically interesting work, but not “almost exclusively,” because effects vary by person and job context.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson — “Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work” (2005).**  
  Meta-analysis showing that person–job fit and person–organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. The key point is not just whether the work is interesting, but whether the role, culture, manager, and trajectory fit you.

- **Deci, Koestner & Ryan — “A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” (1999).**  
  Finds that certain external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This supports caution about choosing mainly for money or status, but it does not imply salary and career structure are irrelevant.

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford — “Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance” (2014).**  
  Meta-analysis showing intrinsic motivation predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity. The evidence says intrinsic interest is very important, but the best outcomes often come from both intrinsic and extrinsic alignment.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich — “The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction” (2010).**  
  Meta-analysis finding that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. For someone already well paid, a small salary increase is unlikely to dominate factors like interest, autonomy, learning, and fit.

- **Gagné & Deci — “Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” (2005).**  
  Reviews evidence that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive high-quality motivation and well-being at work. Interesting research work may satisfy competence and autonomy, while leading a small team may satisfy autonomy and relatedness if the environment is good.

- **Spector — “Perceived Control by Employees: A Meta-Analysis of Studies Concerning Autonomy and Participation at Work” (1986).**  
  Shows that autonomy and participation are associated with higher satisfaction, commitment, and performance, and lower stress. A team-lead role could be valuable if it genuinely increases control rather than just adding coordination burden.

- **Crawford, LePine & Rich — “Linking Job Demands and Resources to Employee Engagement and Burnout” (2010).**  
  Meta-analysis distinguishing challenge demands, which can increase engagement, from hindrance demands, which increase burnout. Leading a team can be motivating if it feels like growth, but costly if it mainly creates bureaucracy, ambiguity, or politics.

- **Christian, Garza & Slaughter — “Work Engagement: A Quantitative Review and Test of Its Relations with Task and Contextual Performance” (2011).**  
  Finds that job resources, meaningfulness, autonomy, and feedback predict engagement, which in turn predicts performance. This supports prioritizing engaging work, but engagement is broader than “interesting technical content” alone.

- **Kahneman & Deaton — “High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” (2010).**  
  Finds that income is more strongly related to life evaluation than day-to-day emotional well-being, with diminishing returns in their data. For high earners, this suggests work quality may matter more than a modest salary bump for daily happiness.

- **Killingsworth — “Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” (2021).**  
  Finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income beyond earlier proposed thresholds. However, the relationship is roughly logarithmic, so small income differences at already-high levels may be less important than large differences in work experience.

- **Bunderson & Thompson — “The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” (2009).**  
  Shows that people with highly meaningful work may accept sacrifices, lower pay, and worse conditions because the work feels like a calling. This warns that “interesting” or “meaningful” work can be valuable but also exploitable if treated as the only criterion.

- **Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones — “What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?” (2012).**  
  Shows that people’s choices are not explained by happiness alone; they also value meaning, purpose, status, control, and identity. This is directly relevant because “more interesting research” and “leading a team” may satisfy different values.

- **Schkade & Kahneman — “Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” (1998).**  
  Demonstrates that people overestimate the importance of whatever feature they are currently focusing on. In your case, friends may overfocus on interesting work, while recruiters may overfocus on salary/title, so neither dimension should dominate alone.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports putting heavy weight on genuinely engaging work, but not on “interestingness” alone.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
A large meta-analysis found that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and work context all predict satisfaction and performance. Interesting technical content matters, but leadership, team quality, autonomy, and support also matter.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation — Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
Work is more motivating and sustainable when it satisfies autonomy, competence, and relatedness. The paper supports choosing work that feels intrinsically meaningful, but it does not support ignoring growth, relationships, or role design.

**Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka & Lanivich (2011)**  
Vocational interests predict performance and turnover, though effects are meaningful rather than overwhelming. This supports taking “I find the work interesting” seriously as a career factor, not treating it as a luxury.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)**  
Interest fit predicts performance in jobs, training, and education across many studies. This suggests that staying close to work you find intellectually compelling can compound into better performance and career capital.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor strongly predicts satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. The relevant question is broader than “Meta interesting vs Google less interesting”: it is overall fit with the work, manager role, team, and culture.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Pay level is positively related to job and pay satisfaction, but the relationship is relatively modest. For already high-paying roles, a small salary increase is unlikely to dominate daily work quality or fit.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006)**  
People tend to overfocus on salient factors like income when predicting future happiness and underweight daily experience. This supports being cautious about choosing mainly for a somewhat higher salary.

**The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions — Grant (2008)**  
Seeing how one’s work benefits others can increase motivation and performance. A team-lead role may create more perceived impact even if the technical problem itself is less interesting.

**Developing Leaders via Experience: The Role of Developmental Challenge, Learning Orientation, and Feedback Availability — DeRue & Wellman (2009)**  
Challenging assignments can accelerate leadership development, especially when feedback is available and the person has a learning orientation. This supports valuing the Google role if leading a team is a core career goal, even if the work is less intellectually exciting.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art — Bakker & Demerouti (2007)**  
Engagement and burnout depend on both job resources, such as autonomy and learning, and job demands, such as workload and emotional strain. Interesting work is a powerful resource, but management duties can add both resources and demands.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
People who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher work and life satisfaction. The paper supports prioritizing deeply meaningful work, but meaning can come from technical discovery, leadership, impact, or identity fit.

answer:
- **On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives** — People tend to overestimate how much extrinsic incentives like pay motivate behavior and underestimate intrinsic motives like interest, learning, and meaning. This supports the claim that interesting work is often underrated, but not that other factors should be ignored.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially for complex and quality-sensitive work. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence argues against focusing on interestingness almost exclusively.

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. Interesting work matters most when paired with autonomy, impact, and clear feedback.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback predict satisfaction, performance, and lower strain. Social support, interdependence, and context also matter, so technical interestingness is only one part of job quality.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Person–job and person–organization fit are strongly related to satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. This suggests evaluating fit with your research interests, leadership goals, values, team, and manager rather than optimizing only one dimension.

- **Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Vocational interests predict performance and persistence, especially when interests closely match the actual work. This supports taking genuine interest seriously for demanding expert roles.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Pay level is positively but modestly related to job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. A somewhat higher salary may matter, but its effect is often smaller than job content, fit, and work conditions.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** — Income strongly predicts life evaluation, but daily emotional well-being showed little improvement beyond a high-income threshold in the original analysis. This supports diminishing returns to extra salary, especially when current compensation is already high.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — This paper reconciles earlier findings by showing that well-being generally keeps rising with income, while plateauing mainly among the least happy subgroup. It cautions against saying money does not matter, but also suggests money may not compensate for poor fit or draining work.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting, especially when rewards feel controlling. The practical issue is whether a new role’s incentives, constraints, and management style would support or crowd out your motivation.

- **Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation** — Autonomy, competence, and relatedness support motivation, performance, persistence, and well-being at work. A small-team lead role could be motivating if it increases these needs, even if the technical domain is less interesting.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — People who experience work as a calling tend to report greater work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. This supports prioritizing meaningful work, while emphasizing that meaning depends on personal identity and framing.

- **The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions** — Perceiving that one’s work has impact on others can increase persistence, effort, and performance. “Interesting” may therefore include leadership impact and user or team outcomes, not only technical novelty.

- **Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity** — Creativity is supported by challenge, freedom, resources, supervisory encouragement, work-group support, and a supportive organizational climate. For research engineering, interesting projects matter greatly but depend on whether the environment protects exploration and autonomy.

- **Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis** — Objective career success, such as pay and promotions, and subjective career success, such as satisfaction, have overlapping but distinct predictors. This supports separating “will this advance my career?” from “will I enjoy and value the work?” rather than treating either as decisive alone.

- **Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — People overestimate the importance of salient differences when imagining future satisfaction. In career choices, salient features like salary bump, company name, or team-lead title may be overweighted relative to daily work content.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)**  
People who view their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those who view it mainly as a job or career. This supports the idea that meaningful, intrinsically valued work can be unusually important, though people can sometimes craft meaning within different roles.

answer:
- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives”** — People tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by pay and external rewards, and underestimate intrinsic motives like interest, meaning, and autonomy. This supports the idea that “interesting work” is often underrated relative to salary/status.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”** — Pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This suggests a somewhat higher salary may matter, but usually less than people expect once basic needs and fairness are met.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion”** — People often overpredict how much higher income will improve day-to-day happiness because income becomes overly salient during decisions. This is relevant when comparing a more interesting job with a somewhat better-paid one.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year”** — Higher income continues to predict higher experienced well-being, including at high income levels. However, the effect is gradual, so a small-to-moderate raise may not dominate differences in work content.

- **“Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory”** — Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” matters, but the paper implies it is one part of a broader job-design package.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”** — Autonomy, task variety, feedback, task significance, and social support are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. The evidence favors optimizing for rich work design, not interest alone.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”** — Well-being and motivation depend heavily on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting work helps mainly when it also supports these needs, while leadership may help if it increases competence, autonomy, and impact.

- **“Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions”** — Intrinsic motivation is linked to persistence, creativity, and well-being, especially for complex work. Extrinsic rewards are not irrelevant, but they are less powerful when the main work requires deep curiosity and creativity.

- **“To Pay or Not to Pay: A Meta-Analysis of the Effects of Incentives on Intrinsic Motivation”** — Controlling external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This cautions against choosing mainly for compensation if the tradeoff is a large loss in intellectual engagement.

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity”** — Creativity is supported by challenging work, autonomy, resources, supervisory encouragement, and supportive teams. For a research-engineering role, the “interestingness” of the work and the local environment both matter for creative output.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?”** — People who believe interests are fixed are more likely to abandon areas when they become difficult or initially less exciting. This suggests a “less interesting” leadership role could become more engaging if it lets you develop new interests.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — Fit with the role, organization, team, and manager predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. The best choice depends on overall fit, not only salary, title, or topic interest.

- **“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review”** — Meaningful work comes from many sources, including self-expression, contribution to others, belonging, and organizational context. Interesting technical work is one source of meaning, while leading a team could be another.

- **“Job Crafting and Cultivating Positive Meaning and Identity in Work”** — People can reshape their tasks, relationships, and interpretation of work to make jobs more meaningful. A less interesting job description may still become fulfilling if the role has enough autonomy and scope.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Test of the Challenge Stressor–Hindrance Stressor Framework”** — Challenge stressors like responsibility and complex goals can increase motivation and performance, though they may also increase strain. Leading a small team could be rewarding if it feels like a challenge rather than bureaucracy or obstruction.

- **“Affective Forecasting”** — People are often poor at predicting how future choices will make them feel because they underestimate adaptation and overfocus on salient features. This matters because salary, title, and “interesting work” may all be misweighted before actually living the role.

answer:
- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis”**  
  Intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially for complex and quality-sensitive work like research. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence supports prioritizing interesting work but not focusing on it “almost exclusively.”

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation”**  
  Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. This suggests that choosing mainly for salary/status can backfire if it moves you away from work you genuinely enjoy.

- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis”**  
  Fit between a person’s interests and their job is reliably associated with job satisfaction. This is direct evidence that “interesting to me” is an important career variable, not a luxury.

- **“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover”**  
  Vocational interests predict job performance and lower turnover. The effects are meaningful but not dominant, so interest should be weighed heavily but alongside other factors.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”**  
  Autonomy, task significance, feedback, social support, and complexity all predict satisfaction and motivation. “Interesting work” often bundles these, but a team-lead role may also increase autonomy, impact, and social rewards.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis”**  
  Meaningful work is strongly linked to engagement, job satisfaction, commitment, and lower withdrawal intentions. If your current research feels meaningful as well as interesting, that is a major point in its favor.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”**  
  Pay has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, especially compared with work characteristics and fit. A somewhat higher salary is unlikely to compensate fully for a large drop in daily interest.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved”**  
  Income is associated with higher emotional well-being for many people, though effects are roughly proportional to log income and vary by baseline happiness. This argues against ignoring salary, but also against treating a modest raise as decisive.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion”**  
  People tend to overestimate how much income affects day-to-day happiness when attention is focused on money. This supports the idea that salient extrinsic gains can be overweighted relative to daily work experience.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This argues against a single-factor rule: interesting tasks matter, but so do culture, manager, team, and role fit.

- **“The Effects of Met Expectations on Newcomer Attitudes and Behaviors: A Review and Meta-Analysis”**  
  When the actual job matches expectations, people show higher satisfaction and commitment and lower turnover. The key question is whether “leading a small team” will really feel as rewarding as expected and whether “less interesting” will be as costly as expected.

- **“Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress”**  
  Leaders showed lower stress than nonleaders, partly because leadership came with greater perceived control. If managing people is a genuine aspiration and brings autonomy, the leadership opportunity may be psychologically meaningful rather than merely extrinsic.

answer:
- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Deci & Ryan**

  Fit between a person’s interests and their work predicts performance, persistence, and satisfaction. The evidence supports taking interesting work seriously, but not treating it as the only factor.

- **“The Social Psychology of Creativity: A Componential Conceptualization” — Amabile**  
  Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creative and research-like work. If your Meta work is unusually creative and Google’s role is routine, the research would put real weight on staying.

- **“Motivational Synergy: Toward New Conceptualizations of Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivation in the Workplace” — Amabile**

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby & Herron**  
  Creativity is supported by challenging work, autonomy, resources, supervisory encouragement, and organizational support. “Interesting problems” matter, but the environment around the problems often matters just as much.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art” — Bakker & Demerouti**  
  Engagement comes from resources such as autonomy, feedback, support, and development opportunities, while burnout comes from excessive demands. A fascinating role can still be bad if it lacks resources or creates chronic strain.

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich**  
  Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. A slightly higher salary alone usually should not dominate a decision if the work, fit, autonomy, or growth path differs substantially.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers**  
  Higher income is associated with higher well-being for many people, though the effect is not uniform and is weaker for some already-unhappy groups. This suggests salary matters, but it is unlikely to compensate fully for a major loss of meaningful or engaging work.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz**  
  People who experience work as a calling tend to report greater satisfaction and meaning. If your current research role feels like a calling, losing that may be psychologically costly.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue**  
  High performers in individual-contributor roles are often promoted into management even when the skills do not transfer well. Wanting to lead a team is a real reason to move, but the research says to evaluate whether you actually like and fit managerial work.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton**  
  Bosses have large effects on worker productivity, partly by coaching and spreading better practices. Leading a small team can create meaningful leverage, especially if you value developing others and shaping direction.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** This classic paper finds that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports prioritizing the day-to-day substance of the work, but defines “interesting” broadly enough to include autonomy and responsibility.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.** This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, and social support are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It suggests that interesting research problems matter a lot, but team leadership could also matter if it increases autonomy, impact, and social connection.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci, 2005.** This paper argues that work is most motivating when it satisfies autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A role that is technically fascinating but low in autonomy or growth can disappoint, while a less technically interesting management role can work if it strongly satisfies those needs.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** The paper finds that expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when rewards feel controlling. It supports being cautious about chasing salary or title at the expense of intrinsically motivating work.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and complex work. It argues against focusing on interesting work “almost exclusively,” because incentives and role structure still matter.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** This paper finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. It supports the idea that, once compensation is already high, marginal salary increases may be less important than job content, fit, and work design.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** This paper finds that emotional well-being generally continues rising with income for many people, though not equally for everyone. It cautions against dismissing salary entirely, but does not imply salary will compensate for uninteresting or poorly fitting work.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** This meta-analysis finds that fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. It suggests the key question is not just “interesting vs. salary,” but whether the role fits your motives, values, team preferences, and desired identity.

- **“The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect” — Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005.** This paper finds that job changes often produce an initial satisfaction boost that fades over time. It is relevant because a new company, higher salary, and leadership title may feel especially attractive before the stable day-to-day reality is clear.

- **“Immune Neglect: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting” — Gilbert, Pinel, Wilson, Blumberg & Wheatley, 1998.** This paper shows that people often overestimate how long future events will affect their happiness. It suggests that salient changes like salary, title, or employer brand may be over-weighted relative to recurring daily experiences like problem quality and autonomy.

- **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001.** This paper argues that employees can reshape tasks, relationships, and meaning in their jobs. A less interesting role may become more interesting if leadership lets you craft the mission and team, while an already interesting research role may be further improved by intentional job crafting.

- **“Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead” — Chan & Drasgow, 2001.** This paper distinguishes different forms of motivation to lead and shows that motivation to lead predicts leadership-related outcomes. If leading a small team is a genuine intrinsic goal for you, it may count as “interesting work” rather than merely an extrinsic promotion.

answer:
- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath, 1999.** People tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by pay and status, and underestimate intrinsic motivation. This supports the “interesting work is underrated” part, but not the “almost exclusively” part.

- **“Work Values and Job Rewards: A Theory of Job Satisfaction” — Kalleberg, 1977.** Job satisfaction depends on the match between what people value and what the job provides, including intrinsic, financial, career, convenience, and social rewards. Intrinsic rewards matter a lot, but the paper explicitly treats them as one category among several.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” is central, but autonomy and feedback are equally built into the theory.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.** This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task significance, feedback, social support, and work context predict satisfaction, commitment, and performance. It argues for prioritizing work design broadly, not intellectual interest alone.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. This supports caring about whether the work genuinely fits you, but also about manager, team, and culture fit.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci, 2000.** Well-being and high-quality motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research may satisfy these needs, but a team-lead role could also satisfy them if it brings real ownership and connection.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile, 1997.** Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creativity and novel problem-solving. The paper strongly supports prioritizing interesting work in research roles, while also emphasizing autonomy, resources, and supervisory climate.

- **“Does Intrinsic Motivation Fuel the Prosocial Fire? Motivational Synergy in Predicting Persistence, Performance, and Productivity” — Grant, 2008.** Intrinsic motivation and prosocial motivation together predict persistence, performance, and productivity. Interesting work is powerful, but purpose and impact can substantially amplify it.

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. A higher salary matters, but the evidence does not suggest it should dominate role quality once pay is already high.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** Emotional well-being generally rises with income for most people, while flattening mainly among the least happy subgroup at higher incomes. This argues against ignoring salary, but also against treating a moderate pay bump as decisive by itself.

- **“Linking Job Demands and Resources to Employee Engagement and Burnout: A Theoretical Extension and Meta-Analytic Test” — Crawford, LePine & Rich, 2010.** Job resources and challenge demands can increase engagement, while hindrance demands predict burnout. A leadership role could be energizing if it brings challenge and resources, or draining if it mostly brings politics, coordination load, and blocked execution.

- **“Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress” — Sherman et al., 2012.** Leaders in the studied samples showed lower cortisol and anxiety than non-leaders, largely explained by greater perceived control. Leading a small team may improve well-being if it provides real control rather than just extra responsibility.

- **“Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” — Schkade & Kahneman, 1998.** People overestimate the importance of whichever life difference is most salient while making a comparison. This cautions against over-weighting any single dimension, including interestingness, salary, or title.

- **“What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?” — Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones, 2012.** People often choose what they think will make them happier, but they also value meaning, status, morality, family, and other non-happiness objectives. This suggests that choosing a less interesting role for leadership growth can be rational even if predicted enjoyment is lower.

answer:
- **On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives — Heath (1999)**  
  The paper finds an “extrinsic incentives bias”: people systematically overestimate how much others are driven by pay and underestimate motives like interesting work, learning, and purpose. It supports the idea that interesting work is often underrated, but only as a bias claim, not as evidence to ignore salary, role scope, or career direction.

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman and Oldham (1976)**  
  Jobs with autonomy, feedback, skill variety, task identity, and task significance generate higher internal motivation and satisfaction, especially for people who want growth. It implies that intellectually rich research work can be valuable, but so can a team-lead role if it increases autonomy, significance, feedback, and growth.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner, and Ryan (1999)**  
  Across experiments, expected tangible rewards often reduce intrinsic motivation for activities people already find interesting, while positive informational feedback can increase it. This cautions against letting a modest compensation gain dominate an intrinsically motivating role, but it does not say compensation is irrelevant.

- **Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation — Gagné and Deci (2005)**  
  The paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central to high-quality work motivation, well-being, and persistence. The relevant comparison is whether Meta research or Google team leadership better satisfies those needs, not whether one is simply more “interesting.”

- **Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby, and Herron (1996)**  
  This empirical work links creativity to challenging work, autonomy, resources, supervisory encouragement, and low organizational impediments. For a research engineer, preserving hard, self-directed problem solving may matter a lot for creative output, while leadership is attractive only if it does not replace all creative work with coordination.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, and Tay (2019)**  
  Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. This supports prioritizing meaning and interest heavily, but meaningfulness can also come from mentoring, leading, impact, and identity fit.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson (2005)**  
  Fit across job tasks, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and intent to stay. The evidence favors evaluating the full role fit—technical interest, management desire, manager, team, culture, and growth path—rather than optimizing a single dimension.

- **Interests, Personality, and Work Performance: A Meta-Analysis — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka, and Lanivich (2011)**  
  Vocational interests are meaningfully related to job performance, training performance, and persistence, especially when interests match job content. This supports taking the “less interesting” part seriously because interest fit can affect both happiness and effectiveness.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich (2010)**  
  Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, and people in richer countries or higher-paid roles are not proportionally more satisfied. This suggests a higher salary is useful but unlikely to compensate for a large loss in interest, autonomy, or fit.

- **The Importance of Pay in Employee Motivation: Discrepancies Between What People Say and What They Do — Rynes, Gerhart, and Minette (2004)**  
  The paper shows people often understate the importance of pay, while behavioral evidence indicates pay strongly influences attraction, retention, and motivation. This is the main counterweight to the “focus almost exclusively on interesting work” claim.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman, and Mellers (2023)**  
  This paper reconciles prior income-well-being findings by showing that emotional well-being generally continues to rise with income, but unevenly and with exceptions among the least happy people. It implies salary increases can matter, though they may matter less than removing a major source of daily dissatisfaction.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, and Schwartz (1997)**  
  The paper distinguishes seeing work as a job, career, or calling, and shows that these orientations are linked to different satisfaction patterns. It suggests the right choice depends partly on whether you experience research excellence, people leadership, or status/advancement as central to your work identity.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham, 1976**  
This foundational job-design paper finds that work is more motivating when it provides skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. It supports the importance of interesting, meaningful work, but frames it as one cluster of job features rather than the only criterion.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson, 2007**  
This meta-analysis shows that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support are strongly associated with satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower stress or turnover. The evidence favors choosing engaging, well-designed work, but also says leadership support, workload, social context, and conditions matter.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being — Ryan & Deci, 2000**  
This review argues that sustained motivation and well-being are driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness, especially when motivation is intrinsic. An interesting research role may score high on competence and autonomy, while a team-lead role may score high on autonomy and relatedness, so the theory does not imply that “interestingness” alone dominates.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford, 2014**  
This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality and complex work, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity. It argues against ignoring either interest or rewards, but suggests intrinsic interest is particularly important for creative and research-heavy roles.

**Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka, & Lanivich, 2011**  
This meta-analysis finds that alignment between a person’s vocational interests and their work predicts job performance and lower turnover. It directly supports taking “interesting to me” seriously in career choice, though the effects are meaningful rather than overwhelming.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson, 2005**  
This meta-analysis finds that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and intention to stay. It suggests the key question is broader fit—work content, team, manager, organization, values—not just salary or abstract prestige.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich, 2010**  
This meta-analysis finds that pay has a positive but relatively modest relationship with job satisfaction. A “bit higher salary” is unlikely, by itself, to dominate differences in day-to-day work quality, autonomy, fit, and meaning.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers, 2023**  
This paper reconciles conflicting findings and concludes that emotional well-being generally rises with income for many people, but not uniformly and often with diminishing practical importance at higher incomes. It weakens the claim that money is irrelevant, while also weakening the claim that a modest raise should outweigh the nature of the work.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art — Bakker & Demerouti, 2007**  
This review argues that engagement and burnout depend on the balance between job resources such as autonomy, feedback, growth, and support and job demands such as workload, pressure, and emotional strain. A team-lead role could be better if it adds resources you value, or worse if it mainly adds coordination burden and less engaging work.

**Job Demands, Job Decision Latitude, and Mental Strain: Implications for Job Redesign — Karasek, 1979**  
This classic paper finds that high-demand jobs are especially harmful when workers have low control, while high-demand and high-control jobs can be more active and developmental. For your choice, the important distinction is not just “interesting versus less interesting” but whether the leadership role gives real decision latitude.

**Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead — Chan & Drasgow, 2001**  
This paper develops the idea that people differ in motivation to lead, including affective enjoyment of leading and willingness to take leadership responsibility. If leading a team is intrinsically motivating for you, the Google role may contain a form of “interesting work” that is not captured by the technical content alone.

**A Wandering Mind Is an Unhappy Mind — Killingsworth & Gilbert, 2010**  
This experience-sampling study finds that people are less happy when their minds wander, and attention to the present activity is a strong correlate of happiness. It implies that daily engagement and absorption in the work may matter more than headline compensation or title.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It? — O’Keefe, Dweck, & Walton, 2018**  
This paper shows that people who see interests as fixed are less likely to explore and more likely to give up when new domains become difficult. It cautions against assuming the Google role will stay uninteresting forever, especially if leadership is a skill domain you genuinely want to develop.

**Understanding Contemporary Forms of Exploitation: Attributions of Passion Serve to Legitimize the Poor Treatment of Workers — Kim, Campbell, Shepherd, & Kay, 2020**  
This paper shows that passion for work can make observers see poorer treatment of workers as more acceptable. It is a caution against focusing on interesting work “almost exclusively,” because passion can make people tolerate bad terms or weak boundaries.

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014), “Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis.”**

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010), “The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature.”**

- **Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, & Stone (2006), “Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion.”**

- **Killingsworth (2021), “Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year.”**

- **Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999), “A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation.”**  
  Certain external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially when they feel controlling. This supports caution about choosing mainly for salary, prestige, or title if it reduces autonomy or curiosity.

- **Grant (2008), “The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions.”**

  Meaningful work can produce deep satisfaction and identity. It can also lead people to tolerate worse conditions, lower pay, and self-exploitation, which cautions against optimizing for interesting work “almost exclusively.”

A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” — Schkade & Kahneman, 1998.** People mispredicted happiness by overfocusing on a salient difference, climate, while underweighting the ordinary structure of daily life. - **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, particularly for complex and quality-focused work. - **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. - **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al., 2010.** Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, and pay satisfaction is not the same as overall work satisfaction. This supports not overweighting a small salary increase, especially if the current salary already meets your needs.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** Experienced well-being generally rises with income, but the relationship differs across people and is not a simple universal plateau. This cautions against saying salary does not matter, while still suggesting that marginal income should be weighed against daily work quality.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019.** Meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal. Meaning can come from intellectually interesting research or from leading and developing a team, so “interesting” and “meaningful” should be separated.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009.** People with a strong calling experience deep meaning but may also tolerate worse pay, sacrifices, and exploitation. This is a direct warning against optimizing for interest or meaning almost exclusively.

- **“What Type of Leadership Behaviors Are Functional in Teams? A Meta-Analysis” — Burke et al., 2006.** Team leadership behaviors are meaningfully related to team performance, with both task-focused and person-focused leadership mattering. If leading a small team is intrinsically important to you, the Google role’s leadership component is a real motivational variable, not merely a title upgrade.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** This paper finds that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports taking the intrinsic interest and structure of the work very seriously.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** This meta-analysis finds that some external rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. It supports the concern that optimizing mainly for pay/status can backfire if it crowds out curiosity and ownership.

- **“The Relation between Interest Congruence and Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis” — Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg, 1993.** This paper finds that fit between a person’s interests and their work environment is positively related to job satisfaction. The effect is meaningful but not so large that interest should be treated as the only criterion.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** This meta-analysis shows that fit with the job, organization, team, and manager predicts satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. It suggests comparing the two roles on total fit, not just “interesting research” versus “salary/team lead.”

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** This paper finds that pay level is only weakly to moderately related to job satisfaction. It supports the idea that a modest salary increase should usually not dominate work content, autonomy, and fit.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006.** This paper argues that people often overestimate the happiness impact of income because salary is salient when comparing options. It supports your friends’ claim that interesting daily work can be underrated relative to easily comparable compensation.

- **“Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation and Separate Evaluation” — Hsee & Zhang, 2004.** This paper shows that when people compare options side by side, they overweight attributes that are easy to compare and underweight attributes that shape lived experience. It is directly relevant because salary/title differences are easier to compare than day-to-day intellectual engagement.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021.** This paper finds that experienced well-being continues to increase with income across a wide range, rather than fully plateauing at a fixed threshold. It cautions against dismissing salary entirely, even if money is not the whole story.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** This paper reconciles earlier conflicting findings by showing that income keeps helping many people, while the least happy group shows more of a plateau. It suggests money matters, but not enough to justify ignoring whether the work itself is energizing.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.** This paper finds that people who experience their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and meaning. It supports prioritizing work that feels intrinsically meaningful, though it does not imply that everyone should optimize exclusively for “interestingness.”

- **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001.** This paper argues that employees can reshape tasks, relationships, and interpretations of their work to create more meaning. It matters here because a less-interesting Google role might become more interesting if the leadership scope lets you craft it.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art” — Bakker & Demerouti, 2007.** This paper argues that resources such as autonomy, feedback, support, and growth opportunities drive engagement, while excessive demands drive burnout. It suggests evaluating both roles on energy, autonomy, support, and stress—not only intellectual interest.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.** This paper finds that good bosses substantially improve worker productivity, showing that management quality has large effects. It makes the team-lead opportunity potentially valuable as a real source of impact and skill growth, not merely a status perk.
